g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of interrupts in 8051 microcontrollers, essential for real-time embedded systems.
An interrupt is a mechanism that temporarily suspends program execution to handle time-critical events immediately.
g.text: A mechanism that temporarily suspends normal program execution to respond to
time-critical events like timer overflow, external signals, or serial communication.
Key Benefit: CPU can perform main tasks while responding to events instantly!

g.data-narration: Compare the polling approach, which wastes CPU cycles, with the efficient interrupt-driven method.
The 8051 has five interrupt sources: two external interrupts, two timer interrupts, and one serial port interrupt.
Each interrupt has a fixed vector address where the CPU jumps when the interrupt is acknowledged.
The IE register at address A8H controls interrupt enabling. EA bit must be set to enable any interrupt.
g.text: EA must be 1 to enable any interrupt!

g.data-narration: The IP register sets priority levels. Higher priority interrupts preempt lower ones when simultaneous.
The CPU completes current instruction, saves PC to stack, jumps to vector, executes ISR, then restores PC.
External interrupts can be edge-triggered or level-triggered using IT0/IT1 bits in TCON register.
g.text: ⚠ Edge-triggering is preferred to avoid multiple triggers!

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates Timer 0 overflow interrupt to toggle an LED every 1ms using 12MHz crystal.
This example configures External Interrupt 0 on falling edge with high priority to increment a counter.
g.text: Configure INT0 on falling edge, high priority, increment counter at RAM 30H

g.data-narration: This example shows multiple interrupts: Timer 1 for keypad scanning and Serial port for data reception.
g.text: Timer 1 (10ms) for keypad scan + Serial port for data reception

g.data-narration: Review the key points: vector addresses, IE/IP registers, and the critical RETI instruction usage.
Remember: always set EA bit, use RETI, clear interrupt flags, and manually save registers in ISRs.
g.text: 1. Forgetting EA=1 bit → No interrupts work
2. Using RET instead of RETI → Undefined behavior
